meta.notes: AI-generated proof-of-concept CPBS items for SpeedyCAT (chunk 10 of 10). NOT real MCAT/AAMC content and NOT from any answer key. Every stem and choice is plain text (no HTML/SVG/LaTeX). Answer keys were computed programmatically and each item passed a 4-criteria eval (four distinct choices; clean plain-text formatting; MCAT-appropriate difficulty; exactly one correct choice).

questions.stem: What is the atomic number of nitrogen?
questions.explanation: The atomic number equals the number of protons; nitrogen has 7.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: In a perfectly inelastic collision, which quantity is conserved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total momentum, while kinetic energy is not fully conserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Kinetic energy is conserved but momentum is not.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

questions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in all collisions with no external force; kinetic energy is conserved only in elastic collisions.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Adding another resistor in parallel causes the total resistance to
questions.explanation: Adding parallel paths gives current more routes, lowering total resistance below the smallest branch.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: A sample starts with 80 g of a radioisotope whose half-life is 2 days. How much remains after 6 days?
questions.explanation: Number of half-lives = 6/2 = 3; remaining = 80 x (1/2)^3 = 10 g.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: According to VSEPR theory, what is the molecular geometry of NF3?
questions.explanation: NF3 adopts a trigonal pyramidal geometry based on its bonding and lone-pair electron domains.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Convert 8 km to meters.
questions.explanation: Apply the SI prefix conversion: 8 km = 8000 m.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: According to Dalton's law, the total pressure of a gas mixture equals

choices.label: A
choices.text: the sum of the partial pressures of the components.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the pressure of the heaviest gas only.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the average of the partial pressures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the product of the partial pressures.

questions.explanation: Dalton's law: total pressure equals the sum of individual partial pressures.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Adding 8360 J of heat to 250 g of water raises its temperature by how much? (c = 4.18 J/(g·°C))
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: A force of 500 N is applied over an area of 5 m^2. What is the pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F / A = 500 / 5 = 100 Pa.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The halogens are found in
questions.explanation: Group 17 (F, Cl, Br, I) are the reactive nonmetals called halogens.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: An object starts at 5 m/s and accelerates uniformly at 9.8 m/s^2 for 4 s. What is its final velocity?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: A current of 2 A flows through a 2 Ω resistor. What is the voltage across it?
questions.explanation: Ohm's law: V = I R = (2)(2) = 4 V.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The law of reflection states that

choices.label: A
choices.text: light slows and bends toward the normal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the angle of incidence is always larger than the angle of reflection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

questions.explanation: For reflection, incidence and reflection angles (from the normal) are equal.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: What is the approximate bond angle in NF3 (trigonal pyramidal)?
questions.explanation: A trigonal pyramidal molecule such as NF3 has bond angles of about 107 degrees.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The SI base unit of mass is the
questions.explanation: The kilogram is the SI base unit of mass.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: What is the molarity of a solution with 2 mol of solute in 1.5 L of solution?
questions.explanation: Molarity M = moles / liters = 2 / 1.5 = 1.33 M.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: A reaction proceeds in two steps with ΔH1 = -100 kJ and ΔH2 = 250 kJ. If the overall reaction is the sum of these steps, what is the overall ΔH?
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, ΔH_overall = ΔH1 + ΔH2 = -100 + 250 = 150 kJ.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: What is the gauge pressure at a depth of 2 m in a fluid of density 13600 kg/m^3? (g = 9.8 m/s^2)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: How many neutrons are in an atom of neon-20? (atomic number 10)
questions.explanation: neutrons = mass number - atomic number = 20 - 10 = 10.

questions.stem: How many valence electrons does a neutral beryllium (Be) atom have?
questions.explanation: beryllium (Be) is in a main group whose number of valence electrons is 2.

questions.stem: What is the approximate molar mass of MgO? (Use H=1, C=12, N=14, O=16, Na=23, Cl=35.5, S=32, K=39, Ca=40, Mg=24, Fe=56, Ag=108)
questions.explanation: Summing the atomic masses in MgO gives a molar mass of about 40 g/mol.

questions.stem: A buffer solution resists changes in pH because it contains

choices.label: C
choices.text: a strong acid and a strong base.

choices.label: D
choices.text: a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid).

questions.explanation: Buffers pair a weak acid/base with its conjugate to neutralize added acid or base.

questions.explanation: Hydrostatic pressure P = ρ g h increases with depth.

questions.stem: How many protons are in an atom of neon-20?
questions.explanation: The number of protons equals the atomic number; for neon-20 that is 10.

questions.stem: Overall, the molecule Cl2 is best described as
questions.explanation: Cl2 is a nonpolar molecule because its symmetric shape makes the bond dipoles cancel, giving no net dipole.

questions.stem: How many moles are in 80 g of MgO? (molar mass 40 g/mol)
questions.explanation: moles = mass / molar mass = 80 / 40 = 2 mol.

questions.stem: What is the molarity of a solution with 3 mol of solute in 1 L of solution?
questions.explanation: Molarity M = moles / liters = 3 / 1 = 3 M.

questions.stem: For an endothermic reaction, increasing the temperature will
questions.explanation: Heat is a 'reactant' for endothermic reactions; raising T shifts toward products and raises K.

questions.stem: What is the density of an object with mass 200 g and volume 20 cm^3?
questions.explanation: density = mass / volume = 200 / 20 = 10 g/cm^3.

questions.stem: What is the mass number of neon-20?
questions.explanation: The mass number counts protons plus neutrons; for neon-20 it is 20.

questions.stem: Adding a nonvolatile solute to a solvent typically

choices.label: A
choices.text: raises the boiling point and lowers the freezing point.

choices.label: B
choices.text: lowers the boiling point and raises the freezing point.

choices.label: C
choices.text: raises both the boiling and freezing points.

questions.explanation: Colligative effects: boiling-point elevation and freezing-point depression.

questions.stem: What is the mass percent of H in NH3?

questions.stem: What is the atomic number of fluorine?

questions.stem: Resistors of 12 Ω, 18 Ω are connected in series. What is the total resistance?

questions.stem: As temperature increases, the solubility of most solid solutes in water
questions.explanation: Most solid solutes become more soluble as temperature rises (gases behave oppositely).

questions.stem: A reaction proceeds in two steps with ΔH1 = 300 kJ and ΔH2 = 250 kJ. If the overall reaction is the sum of these steps, what is the overall ΔH?
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, ΔH_overall = ΔH1 + ΔH2 = 300 + 250 = 550 kJ.

questions.stem: The Pauli exclusion principle states that

choices.label: C
choices.text: orbitals of equal energy are each singly filled before pairing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: no two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers.

questions.explanation: The Pauli principle limits each orbital to two electrons with opposite spins.

questions.stem: When light passes from air into water, it

choices.label: A
choices.text: continues without changing direction or speed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: slows down and bends away from the normal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: slows down and bends toward the normal.

choices.label: D
choices.text: speeds up and bends toward the normal.

questions.explanation: Entering a denser medium (higher n), light slows and refracts toward the normal (Snell's law).
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Ionic compounds tend to have

choices.label: A
choices.text: no ability to conduct electricity in any state.

choices.label: B
choices.text: low melting points and conduct as solids.

choices.label: C
choices.text: very low boiling points and soft crystals.

choices.label: D
choices.text: high melting points and conduct electricity when molten or dissolved.

questions.explanation: Strong lattice forces give high melting points; mobile ions conduct when molten or dissolved.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: An empirical formula represents

choices.label: A
choices.text: the actual number of atoms in one molecule.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the arrangement of atoms in space.

choices.label: C
choices.text: the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the mass of one mole of the compound.

questions.explanation: The empirical formula gives the smallest integer ratio; the molecular formula gives actual counts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Molarity is defined as

choices.label: A
choices.text: moles of solute per kilogram of solvent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: grams of solute per liter of solvent.

choices.label: C
choices.text: liters of solution per mole of solute.

choices.label: D
choices.text: moles of solute per liter of solution.

questions.explanation: Molarity (M) = moles solute / liters solution; moles per kg solvent is molality.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Increasing the concentration of a reactant generally

choices.label: C
choices.text: has no effect on the rate.

questions.explanation: More reactant particles mean more frequent effective collisions, raising the rate.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content
